OLICY
 
 
  POL 002 / v 2.0:  September 2019
 
 
Global Alliance for Genomics and Health:
 
 
Consent Policy
 
Preamble
 
This Policy aims to guide the sharing of genomic and health-related data in a way that supports
 
the autonomous decision-making of data subjects while promoting the common good of
 
international data sharing, allowing everyone to share in the benefits of scientific progress and its
 
applications as is their right. It builds on the GA4GH 
​
Framework for Responsible Sharing of
 
Genomic and Health-Related Data
​
 (“
​
Framework
​
”), and should be read in combination with
 
other GA4GH Policies.
 
 
 
I.
Context
 
 
1.
Background
​
. Informed consent is a bedrock principle underlying the ethical conduct of
 
clinical practice and health research involving humans. Seeking data subjects’ consent to
 
international data sharing shows respect for their abi

f
 
clinical practice and health research involving humans. Seeking data subjects’ consent to
 
international data sharing shows respect for their ability to make their own decisions, and
 
upholds the trust essential for the effective practice of medicine and research. Consent, in any
 
form it takes, will only be valid if freely given by a capable data subject (or the legal
 
representative) who has had enough time, materials, and support to make an informed
 
decision. Data may be accessed and re-used long after consent has been given, yet it may not
 
be possible to fully specify the scope of data sharing at the time of that consent. Also, if the
 
consent requested is too specific, or presented too vaguely, it may be difficult to share data in
 
a way that respects the data subject’s expectations. Like the 
​
Framework
​
, this policy
 
addresses both sharing of prospective (not yet

data in
 
a way that respects the data subject’s expectations. Like the 
​
Framework
​
, this policy
 
addresses both sharing of prospective (not yet collected) data, as well as retrospective
 
(already collected) data. It promotes the sharing of data in a manner consistent with the data
 
subjects’ consent, authorization by competent authorities, and/or lawful bases, so as to
 
comply with applicable laws and general ethical principles.
 
 
2.
Purpose. 
​
This policy describes how to maximize the responsible and respectful international
 
data sharing through the design of consents for prospective data collection and through the
 
assessment of existing consents for retrospective data sharing.
 
 
3.
Principles
​
.
 
 
This Policy is founded on the following basic principles:
 
i.
Consent is an open, communicative, and continuing relationship.
 
1

​
C
ONSENT
 P
OLICY
 
 
ii.
The processes for taking consent and any ongoing communications between
 
researchers and data subjects should be straight-forward, easy to use, and accessible
 
to all.
 
iii.
The consent process should be situated within and overseen by appropriate
 
governance mechanisms.
 
iv.
To the extent possible, the consent process should clearly describe to data subjects
 
any plans to share data across research groups, institutions, sectors (e.g., research,
 
clinical, and commercial), and borders, and any associated limitations or conditions.
 
iii.
Best practices and technologies should be adopted to ensure commitments made to
 
data subjects concerning data sharing are captured, tracked, and communicated
 
across data sharing networks.
 
 
3.
Definitions
​
.
 
 
The following definitions are intended to align with the 
​
Framework
​
 and other GA4GH policies.
 
T

ta sharing networks.
 
 
3.
Definitions
​
.
 
 
The following definitions are intended to align with the 
​
Framework
​
 and other GA4GH policies.
 
They are not intended as a substitute for definitions found in relevant laws or regulations.
 
“
​
anonymized data
​
” means data that are rendered anonymous in such a way that the data subject
 
is not or is no longer identifiable.
 
 
“
​
controlled access
​
” means a data access model whereby qualified researchers apply for data
 
access and their research plans are reviewed, often by a committee. Also known as managed or
 
restricted access.
 
 
“
​
data subject
​
” means the individual whose data have been processed, i.e., collected, generated,
 
held, used, or shared.
 
 
“
​
pseudonymized data
​
” means data which have been processed in such a manner (e.g. by
 
assigning one or more random codes) that the data can no longer be attribu

d data
​
” means data which have been processed in such a manner (e.g. by
 
assigning one or more random codes) that the data can no longer be attributed to a specific data
 
subject without the use of additional information, provided that such additional information is
 
kept separately and is subject to technical and organizational measures to ensure that the data are
 
not attributed to an identified or identifiable natural person. Also known as coded data.
 
 
“
​
registered access
​
” means a data access model whereby qualified researchers apply for data
 
access to one dataset or multiple datasets at once by providing details of their identity for
 
authentication and agreeing to terms and conditions of data use during the registration process.
 
 
II.
Consent Best Practices
 
2

​
C
ONSENT
 P
OLICY
 
 
 
These Best Practices are founded on the Core Elements of the 
​
Framework
​
 and should be
 
interpreted in a proportionate manner that acknowledges different levels of risk for different data
 
types, data sharing contexts, and cultures.
 
Transparency
 
•
Consent materials and discussions should clearly indicate if data may be shared, for
 
example, outside of institutions, across borders, for long-term deposition in repositories,
 
or for commercial purposes, where applicable.
 
•
It should be clear what forms data will take, the sources from which the data have been
 
or will be derived, and with whom and for what purposes data will be shared.
 
•
Consent materials and discussions should make it clear that while every reasonable
 
effort will be made to protect privacy, a risk of re-identifying individuals from their data
 
remains, especially if the data su

e every reasonable
 
effort will be made to protect privacy, a risk of re-identifying individuals from their data
 
remains, especially if the data subjects have already made their data available in other
 
forms, such as public platforms (e.g., genealogy websites).
 
•
Individuals should be given the option to opt-out of data sharing plans without affecting
 
their care or participation in research. However, if this is not possible due to the nature
 
and goals of the activity, this should be made clear.
 
•
Where it may be impractical or impossible to retrieve or destroy data once shared, this
 
should also be specified.
 
•
Once shared, details on how data have been shared and with whom should be made
 
available to data subjects, as well as any general research results. This information
 
should also be shared with the public to the greatest extent possible, in order to highlight
 
t

l as any general research results. This information
 
should also be shared with the public to the greatest extent possible, in order to highlight
 
the benefits of data sharing to advance health research.
 
•
Best practice guidance and available tools should be used in the development of consent
 
materials to support data subject understanding.
 
Accountability
 
•
The consent process, in any form it takes, should be properly documented and subject to
 
quality control measures, addressing versioning of consent, the mapping of data subjects
 
to respective versions, options chosen or withdrawn, and the communication tools used
 
to ensure data are shared as agreed.
 
 
•
Data sharing plans and consent materials should be regularly reviewed, updated, and
 
made available, according to applicable regulations and policies.
 
3

​
C
ONSENT
 P
OLICY
 
 
•
Easy to use and accessible procedures and instructions should be in place for researchers
 
and data subjects to contact each other with updates, questions regarding data sharing,
 
withdrawal, complaints, or inquiries about policies and practices relating to the sharing
 
of genomic and health-related data.
 
 
•
Data sharing plans and consent materials should anticipate if a data subjects’ legal status
 
might change (e.g., when a minor reaches majority or when a data subject loses capacity
 
or passes away) to ensure the future availability of data while respecting data subjects’
 
choices.
 
•
Consent requirements should not unnecessarily exclude individuals with diminished
 
capacity, or individuals who are deceased, from participating in data sharing as long as
 
appropriate protections are in place (e.g., oversight, representation).
 
 
•
Consent material

ceased, from participating in data sharing as long as
 
appropriate protections are in place (e.g., oversight, representation).
 
 
•
Consent materials and discussions should be adapted to support the appropriate
 
involvement of individuals with diminished capacity in decision-making.
 
Privacy, Data Protection, and Confidentiality
 
•
Consent materials and discussions should specify what categories of data will be shared
 
(e.g., DNA analyses, health record data), the processes used to de-identify those data
 
(e.g., rendering data pseudonymized or anonymized), and the governance and security
 
provisions in place to protect those data (e.g., controlled access, registered access, secure
 
computing environments).
 
 
•
If applicable, consent materials and discussions should mention if data may be shared
 
with parties in countries where there is an increased privacy risk and provide in

ent materials and discussions should mention if data may be shared
 
with parties in countries where there is an increased privacy risk and provide information
 
on any potential implications or safety measures being taken.
 
•
Consent materials and discussions should acknowledge that data sharing may have
 
implications for family members.
 
III.
Retrospective Data Sharing
 
•
Prior to retrospective data sharing or a substantial change to data sharing plans, the
 
following review process should be conducted:
 
 
•
The existing consent materials and associated policy documents should be assessed
 
to determine if (broader) data sharing was foreseen.
 
•
If (broader) data sharing was not foreseen, data subjects should where possible and
 
practical be given an opportunity to re-consent to (broader) data sharing, or they
 
should be notified and given an opportunity to opt-out.
 
 
4

​
C
ONSENT
 P
OLICY
 
 
•
If re-consent or notification with opt-out are not possible or practical, it may still be
 
appropriate to share a limited, anonymized version of the data, or to seek
 
authorization from a competent authority (e.g., consent waiver).
 
 
•
Regardless, careful consideration should be given to whether or not (broader) data
 
sharing is justifiable based on the potential risks and benefits.
 
 
•
Data sharing consortia or repositories should provide tools and guidance to allow
 
contributors to assess if their consent materials cover the desired level of data sharing.
 
They should also request attestations or demonstrations from contributors that data
 
sharing is permitted.
 
IV.
Implementation Mechanisms and Amendments
 
 
1.
Organizations and bodies supporting this Policy should take all reasonable and appropriate
 
measures, whether of a regulatory, contractua

ents
 
 
1.
Organizations and bodies supporting this Policy should take all reasonable and appropriate
 
measures, whether of a regulatory, contractual, administrative or other character, to give
 
effect to this Policy and promote its implementation, monitoring, and enforcement.
 
Procedures and policies should be transparent and accessible. Attention should be paid to the
 
interrelation of this Policy with other GA4GH Policies (e.g., Data Privacy and Security
 
Policy, Accountability Policy).
 
 
2.
Any persons, organizations or bodies supporting this Policy may propose one or more
 
amendments to the present Policy by communicating the amendments to the GA4GH’s
 
Regulatory and Ethics Work Stream (REWS). The REWS shall publicly circulate such
 
amendments for comments and possible inclusion in this Policy.
 
 
3.
The REWS, in collaboration with biomedical, patient advocacy, and ethic

e such
 
amendments for comments and possible inclusion in this Policy.
 
 
3.
The REWS, in collaboration with biomedical, patient advocacy, and ethical and policy
 
organizations and committees, will routinely review its provisions, be aware of advances in
 
basic research and technology, and ethical and legal developments, and attempt to ensure that
 
this Policy is fit for purpose.
 
 
V.
Acknowledgements
 
 
This Policy was developed by the Regulatory and Ethics Work Stream of the GA4GH, and is the
 
result of the collaborative work, comments, and input of many individual and organizational
 
contributors.
 
 
5

Framework for Responsible Sharing of Genomic and Health -Related Data  
1   
 
Version: 3 September 2019  Framework for Responsible Sharing of Genomic and Health-­­Related Data Preamble The sharing of genomic and health-related data for biomedical research is of key importance in ensuring continued progress in our understanding of human health and wellbeing. The challenges raised by international, collaborative research require a principled but nevertheless practical Framework that brings together regulators, funders, patient groups, information technologists, industry, publishers, and research consortia to share principles about data exchange. Such a Framework will facilitate responsible research conduct. This Framework is developed under the auspices of the Global Alliance for Genomics and Health. Its mission is to accelerate progress in human health by helping to establish a common Fr

the auspices of the Global Alliance for Genomics and Health. Its mission is to accelerate progress in human health by helping to establish a common Framework of harmonized approaches to enable effective and responsible sharing of genomic and clinical data and to catalyze data sharing projects that drive and demonstrate the value of data sharing. This Framework provides guidance for the responsible sharing of human genomic and health-related data, including personal health data and other types of data that may have predictive power in relation to health. In particular, it highlights, and is guided by, Article 27 of the 1948 Universal Declaration of Human Rights. Article 27 guarantees the rights of every individual in the world “to share in scientific advancement and its benefits” (including to freely engage in responsible scientific inquiry), and at the same time “to the protection of the

cientific advancement and its benefits” (including to freely engage in responsible scientific inquiry), and at the same time “to the protection of the moral and material interests resulting from any scientific…production of which [a person] is the author.” (As set out in Appendix 1, many other international conventions and national laws, regulations, codes and policies also guide responsible data sharing behavior). This Framework is guided by the human rights of privacy, non-discrimination and procedural fairness. At the same time, it considers all human rights principles relevant, complementary and interrelated, founded as they are on respect for human dignity. Since science proceeds only with the broad support of society, respect for all persons is a primary driver underlying all other derived principles. In particular, this Framework establishes a set of foundational principles for re

persons is a primary driver underlying all other derived principles. In particular, this Framework establishes a set of foundational principles for responsible research conduct and oversight of research data systems in the realm of genomic and health-related data sharing. It interprets the right of all people to share in the benefits of scientific progress and its applications as being the duty of data producers and users to engage in responsible scientific inquiry and to access and share genomic and health-related data across the translation continuum, from basic research through practical applications. It recognizes the rights of data producers and users to be recognized for their contributions to research, balanced by the rights of those who donate their data. In addition to being founded on the right of all citizens in all countries to the benefits of the advancements of science, and

who donate their data. In addition to being founded on the right of all citizens in all countries to the benefits of the advancements of science, and on the right of attribution of scientists, it also reinforces the right of scientific freedom. The value of this Framework is that it: offers political and legal dimensions that reach beyond the moral appeals of bioethics and provides a more robust governance framework for genomic and health- related data sharing; speaks to groups and institutions, not just individuals; stresses the progressive

Framework for Responsible Sharing of Genomic and Health -Related Data  
2   
 
realization of duties; and urges action by governments, industry, funders, publishers, and researchers to create an international environment for responsibly sharing data. This Framework will be elaborated by subsequent Policies (Appendix 2) on particular issues such as ethical governance, consent, privacy and security. The Framework and its subsequent Policies should be used in projects around the world (whether Global Alliance “inspired” or not) such that they become the tools that approval entities, recognized by different jurisdictions, will turn or refer to for guidance. Recognizing diversity of legal and ethical approaches and being responsive to emerging issues, both this Framework and its Policies are intended to provide leadership in this domain for wider discussion.   I. Purpose and Interpretation  1

ssues, both this Framework and its Policies are intended to provide leadership in this domain for wider discussion.   I. Purpose and Interpretation  1. Purpose. The purpose of this Framework is to provide a principled and practical framework for the responsible sharing of genomic and health-related data. Its primary goals are to: i. Protect and promote the welfare, rights, and interests of individuals from around the world in genomic and health-related data sharing, particularly those who contribute their data for biomedical research; ii. Complement laws and regulations on privacy and personal data protection, as well as policies and codes of conduct for the ethical governance of research; iii. Foster responsible data sharing and oversight of research data systems;  iv. Establish a framework for greater international data sharing, collaboration and good governance; v. Serve as a dynamic

of research data systems;  iv. Establish a framework for greater international data sharing, collaboration and good governance; v. Serve as a dynamic instrument that can respond to future developments in the science, technology, and practices of genomic and health-related data sharing; vi. Serve as a tool for the evaluation of responsible research by research ethics committees and data access committees; and vii. Provide overarching principles to be respected in developing legally-binding tools such as data access agreements. 2. Interpretation. Without ascribing legal meaning, this Framework should be interpreted in good faith and is to be understood as a whole. The Foundational Principles and Core Elements are to be understood as complementary and interrelated, as appropriate and relevant in different contexts, countries and cultures. This Framework will be supported by Policies for gui

tary and interrelated, as appropriate and relevant in different contexts, countries and cultures. This Framework will be supported by Policies for guidance in particular issues such as, but not limited to, ethical governance, privacy and security, and consent. For the purposes of this Framework, “data sharing” includes data transfer or data exchange between data users, or where data are made available to secondary researchers, either openly or under specified access conditions.

Framework for Responsible Sharing of Genomic and Health -Related Data  
3   
 
Foundational Principles for Responsible Sharing of 
Genomic and Health -Related Data  
• Respect Individuals, Families and  Communities  
• Advance Research and Scientific  Knowledge  
• Promote Health, Wellbeing and the Fair Distribution of  Benefits  
• Foster Trust, Integrity and  Reciprocity   II. Application  This Framework is intended for all entities or individuals providing, storing, accessing, managing or otherwise using genomic and health-related data, including data donors, users, and producers. This includes, but is not limited to, researchers, research participants and patient communities, publishers, research funding agencies, data protection authorities, hospitals, research ethics committees, industry, ministries of health, and public health organizations.   III. Foundational Principles  The Fou

ities, hospitals, research ethics committees, industry, ministries of health, and public health organizations.   III. Foundational Principles  The Foundational Principles of this Framework guide the responsible sharing of genomic and health- related data. They also facilitate compliance with the obligations and norms set by international and national law and policies.   
  IV. Core Elements of Responsible Data Sharing  It is good practice for those involved in genomic and health-related data sharing to have core elements of responsible data sharing in place. The following Core Elements of the Framework aid in the interpretation of the Foundational Principles to individuals and organizations involved in the sharing of genomic and health-related data. The Core Elements should be interpreted in a proportionate manner that acknowledges different levels of risk and community cultural practice

ted data. The Core Elements should be interpreted in a proportionate manner that acknowledges different levels of risk and community cultural practices. This Framework applies to use of data that have been consented to by donors (or their legal representatives) and/or approved for use by competent bodies or institutions in compliance with national and international laws, general ethical principles, and best practice standards that respect restrictions on downstream uses. Endorsement of the Framework does not preclude the development of particular guidance via Policies for specific populations (e.g. children) or issues (e.g. ethical governance, privacy and security, and consent).

Framework for Responsible Sharing of Genomic and Health -Related Data  
4   
 
  Core Elements of Responsible Data Sharing € Transparency  • Develop clearly defined and accessible information on the purposes, processes, procedures and governance frameworks for data sharing. Such information should be presented in a way that is understandable and accessible in both digital and non-digital formats.  • Provide clear information on the purpose, collection, use and exchange of genomic and health-related data, including, but not limited to: data transfer to third parties; international transfer of data; terms of access; duration of data storage; identifiability of individuals and data and limits to anonymity or confidentiality of data; communication of results to individuals and/or groups; oversight of downstream uses of data; commercial involvement; proprietary claims; and processes of withdr

ion of results to individuals and/or groups; oversight of downstream uses of data; commercial involvement; proprietary claims; and processes of withdrawal from data sharing.  • Implement procedures for fairly determining requests for data access and/or exchange.  € Accountability • Put in place systems for data sharing that respect this Framework. • Track the chain of data access and/or exchange to its source. • Develop processes to identify and manage conflicts of interest. • Implement mechanisms for handling complaints related to data misuse; for identifying, reporting and managing breaches; and for instituting appropriate sanctions.  € Engagement • Develop mechanisms to enable citizens to make meaningful contributions to biomedical research and to partake in deliberation on how these contributions can be respected.  • Facilitate deliberation about the wider societal implications of ge

rch and to partake in deliberation on how these contributions can be respected.  • Facilitate deliberation about the wider societal implications of genomic and health-related data sharing among all stakeholders, especially citizens.  € Data Quality and Security • Store and process the data collected, used and transferred in a way that is accurate, verifiable, unbiased, proportionate, and current, so as to enhance their interoperability and replicability and also preserve their long-term searchability and integrity.  • Ensure feedback mechanisms on the utility, quality, security, and accuracy of data, and their annotations, with a view to improving quality and interoperability and appropriate re-use by others.

Framework for Responsible Sharing of Genomic and Health -Related Data  
5   
 
 • Establish proportionate data security measures that mitigate the risk of unauthorized access, data loss and misuse.  • Understand the issues related to lawful requests for data based on law enforcement, public health, or national security concerns.  € Privacy, Data Protection and Confidentiality • Comply with applicable privacy and data protection regulations at every stage of data sharing, and be in a position to provide assurances to citizens that confidentiality and privacy are appropriately protected when data are collected, stored, processed, and exchanged. Privacy and data protection safeguards should be proportionate to the nature and use of the data, whether identifiable, coded or anonymized.  • Forego any attempt to re-identify anonymized data unless where expressly authorized by law.  € Risk-Benef

hether identifiable, coded or anonymized.  • Forego any attempt to re-identify anonymized data unless where expressly authorized by law.  € Risk-Benefit Analysis • Consider the realistic harms and benefits of data sharing on and with individuals, families and communities, including opportunity costs associated with both sharing and not sharing data. Potential realistic benefits may include development of new scientific knowledge and applications, enhanced efficiency, reproducibility and safety of research projects or processes, and more informed decisions about health care. Potential realistic harms may include invasions of privacy or breach of confidentiality and invalid conclusions about research projects.  • Conduct data sharing with a view towards minimizing harms and maximizing benefits to not just those who contribute their data, but also to society and health care systems as a who

iew towards minimizing harms and maximizing benefits to not just those who contribute their data, but also to society and health care systems as a whole, particularly where data pertains to disadvantaged people. Benefits arising from data sharing may not be uniformly distributed throughout communities around the world and may depend on the usability of data within a specified context, national priorities, as well as a specific community’s concern about health and interpretation of wellbeing.  • Undertake a proportionate assessment of the benefits and risks of harm in data sharing, which is periodically monitored according to the reasonable foreseeability of such harms and benefits. Such an assessment may also incorporate mechanisms that track subsequent harms, should they materialize, so as to help inform future policy.  € Recognition and Attribution • Design systems of data sharing with

ck subsequent harms, should they materialize, so as to help inform future policy.  € Recognition and Attribution • Design systems of data sharing with a view towards recognition and attribution that are meaningful and appropriate to the medium or discipline concerned and which provide due credit and acknowledgement of all who contributed to the results.  • Extend recognition and attribution both to primary purposes and, as appropriate, to secondary or downstream uses and applications. All parties should act in good faith to ensure that any connections to original sources of data are maintained where appropriate, to

Framework for Responsible Sharing of Genomic and Health -Related Data  
6   
 
the extent permissible by law.  € Sustainability • Ensure, where appropriate, the sustainability of the data generated for future use, through both archiving and using appropriate identification and retrieval systems, and through critical appraisal of the mechanisms and systems used for sharing genomic and health- related data.  € Education and Training • Dedicate education and training resources so as to advance data sharing and data management and to constantly improve data quality and integrity. Education and training resources should also be dedicated to: fostering and maintaining good records about the effects and impact of data sharing; raising awareness about national health priorities and distribution of health services; building capacity and data sharing infrastructure in countries; and, working towar

out national health priorities and distribution of health services; building capacity and data sharing infrastructure in countries; and, working towards the building of an evidence base about the advantages and potential limitations of data sharing.  € Accessibility and Dissemination • Make reasonable efforts to maximize the accessibility of data for research through lawful and proportionate data sharing.  • Promote collaborative partnerships and data sharing that can generate maximum benefit, along with the harmonization of deposit, management and access procedures and use as a means to promote accessibility.  • Seek to make data and research results widely available, including through publication and digital dissemination, whether positive, negative or inconclusive, depending on the nature and use of the data. Dissemination of data and research results should be conducted in a way that

e, negative or inconclusive, depending on the nature and use of the data. Dissemination of data and research results should be conducted in a way that both promotes scientific collaboration, reproducibility and broad access to data, and yet minimizes obstacles to data sharing while minimizing harms and maximizing benefits to individuals, families and communities.   V. Implementation Mechanisms and Amendments  1. This Framework should be adopted by organizations and bodies involved in genomic and health- related data sharing. Organizations and bodies adhering to this Framework should take all reasonable and appropriate measures, whether of a regulatory, contractual, administrative or other character, to give effect to the Foundational Principles and Core Elements set out in this Framework in accordance with the international law of human rights and should, by means of all reasonable and a

ples and Core Elements set out in this Framework in accordance with the international law of human rights and should, by means of all reasonable and appropriate measures, promote their implementation. 2. Any persons, organizations or bodies adhering to this Framework may propose one or more amendments to the present Framework by communicating the amendments to the Regulatory and

Framework for Responsible Sharing of Genomic and Health -Related Data  
7   
 
Ethics Working Group of the Global Alliance for Genomics and Health (the “REWG”). The REWG shall publicly circulate such amendments for comments and possible inclusion in this Framework. 3. The REWG, in collaboration with biomedical, patient advocacy, and ethical and policy organizations and committees, will track the adoption of this Framework and its application through subsequent Policies. It will also routinely review its provisions, be aware of advances in basic research and technology, and ethical and legal developments, and attempt to ensure that this Framework is fit for purpose.   VI. Acknowledgements  This Framework for Responsible Sharing of Genomic and Health-Related Data is the result of the work of many people and committees. Developed under the auspices of the Global Alliance for Genomics and He

d Health-Related Data is the result of the work of many people and committees. Developed under the auspices of the Global Alliance for Genomics and Health, the Framework was initially formulated by an international committee (Regulatory and Ethics Working Group) representing a wide spectrum of the bioethics, genomics, and clinical communities. Collaborative input was provided from individuals as well as biomedical, patient advocacy, and ethical, policy and legal organizations, committees, and projects from all regions of the world. These include, but are not limited to: • Biobank Standardisation and Harmonisation for Research Excellence project (BioSHaRE); • Centre for Law and Genetics (University of Tasmania); • Centre of Genomics and Policy (McGill University); • ELSI 2.0; • H3Africa; • Health Research Authority (UK); • HeLEX (University of Oxford); • Human Variome Project (HVP); • INS

cy (McGill University); • ELSI 2.0; • H3Africa; • Health Research Authority (UK); • HeLEX (University of Oxford); • Human Variome Project (HVP); • INSERM; • International Cancer Genome Consortium (ICGC); • International Rare Disease Research Consortium (IRDiRC); • International Society for Biological and Environmental Repositories (ISBER); • Personal Genome Project (USA); • PErsonalised Risk Stratification for Prevention and Early deteCTIon of breast cancer project (PERSPECTIVE); • PHG Foundation (UK); • Public Population Project in Genomics and Society-International Policy interoperability and data Access Clearinghouse (P3G-IPAC); and • other Global Alliance for Genomics and Health Working Groups and Task Teams.

License: GA4GH product documentation (public)
Tier: P1 | Jurisdiction: GA4GH, EU, INTL | Framework: GA4GH

DISCLAIMER: Summary for RegBot corpus ingest. Align consent language with institutional ethics review and applicable law (including GDPR where relevant).

Purpose

The Machine Readable Consent Guidance (MRCG), co-developed by GA4GH DURI and REWS Work Streams, explains how to draft consent forms that map to the Data Use Ontology (DUO). Machine-readable consent supports automated, consistent data access decisions while respecting participant authorisations.

Why consent codes matter for cross-border sharing

When genomic datasets move across institutions and borders, Data Access Committees (DACs) must verify that

ent codes matter for cross-border sharing

When genomic datasets move across institutions and borders, Data Access Committees (DACs) must verify that a researcher's intended use aligns with participant consent. Ambiguous consent language increases review burden and transfer risk. DUO provides standard codes specifying permitted uses (e.g. general research use, disease-specific, commercial use restrictions, geographic limits).

MRCG three-part structure

1. Short consent clause — summary description of data use terms in plain language for participants.
2. Detailed explanation — expands the meaning of each data use term.
3. Optional appendix — maps consent language to DUO codes for machine-readable attachment to datasets.

Link to GDPR and broad consent

GA4GH GDPR Briefs note that human-readable consent using standard language on data sharing and linkage is a prerequisite for translating

oad consent

GA4GH GDPR Briefs note that human-readable consent using standard language on data sharing and linkage is a prerequisite for translating consent into machine-readable form. Broad consent for genomic research may be GDPR-permissible when supplemented with clear categories of uses, safeguards, and recipient types — MRCG/DUO operationalises those categories for access control.

Practical checks for consent / DUL review

- Does the consent clause specify whether international sharing is permitted?
- Are secondary/future uses described at an appropriate level of generality with ethics oversight?
- Can each restriction (commercial use, publication moratorium, geographic limit) be mapped to a DUO term?
- Are withdrawal and recontact procedures stated?

Related GA4GH products: Consent Policy (P0), Consent Toolkit clauses, DUO (DURI Work Stream).

GA4GH GDPR Forum Policy Brief — Is "consent for genomic and health-related research" specific enough under the GDPR?
Source: https://www.ga4gh.org/news_item/gdpr-brief-is-consent-for-genomic-and-health-related-research-specific-enough-to-constitute-a-valid-consent-under-the-gdpr/
Author: Edward Dove (University of Edinburgh) | Date: 2 Dec 2019
License: GA4GH GDPR Brief (public; not legal advice)
Tier: P1 | Jurisdiction: EU | Framework: GDPR

DISCLAIMER: Excerpted for RegBot corpus ingest. Not legal advice.

Summary

There is emerging consensus in the genomic research community that "broad consent" is ethically and legally permissible, subject to external and ongoing oversight (e.g. ethics committee approval and monitoring). Broad consent means a participant explicitly consents to use of their data and/or samples for a defined range of future research projects.

GDPR consent definition (A

ans a participant explicitly consents to use of their data and/or samples for a defined range of future research projects.

GDPR consent definition (Article 4(11))

Consent is "any freely given, specific, informed and unambiguous indication of the data subject's wishes by which he or she, by a statement or by a clear affirmative action, signifies agreement to the processing of personal data relating to him or her."

Articles 6(1)(a) and 9(2)(a) require consent for one or more specific (Art 6) or specified (Art 9) purposes. At first glance this might suggest broad consent such as "consent for genomic and health-related research" is insufficient.

Arguments supporting broad consent under GDPR

- Recital 33 permits consent to certain areas of scientific research when subject to generally accepted ethical standards and ethics committee assessment.
- Under purpose limitation (Article 5(1)(b))

scientific research when subject to generally accepted ethical standards and ethics committee assessment.
- Under purpose limitation (Article 5(1)(b)), further processing for scientific research is for "specified, explicit and legitimate purposes."
- "Scientific research" and especially "genomic and health-related research" may constitute a well-defined purpose without listing every future sub-study.
- Article 29 Working Party (WP29) guidance: purposes that cannot be fully specified at the outset may be described at a more general level (Recital 33 exception), though a well-described purpose must still appear in consent.

Explicit consent and future controllers

Article 9 requires explicit consent for special category data. Explicitness relates to evidencing consent, not necessarily narrowing scope. A participant may agree to a research project describing a broad methodology with safegua

ates to evidencing consent, not necessarily narrowing scope. A participant may agree to a research project describing a broad methodology with safeguards for unknown future applications.

Recital 42 requires the data subject to be aware at least of the identity of the controller. For unknown future recipients, naming categories of controllers and processors may suffice where individual researchers cannot yet be identified.

Conclusion for reviewers

A reasonable interpretation is that "consent for genomic and health-related research" likely is specific enough under the GDPR, provided broad consent is supplemented by:
- categories of data-processing methods;
- safeguards mitigating risks of those methods;
- categories of potential data recipients (controllers/processors).

Relevant GDPR provisions: Recital 33, Recital 42, Article 4(11), Article 5(1)(b), Article 6(1)(a), Article 9(2)(a).

ients (controllers/processors).

Relevant GDPR provisions: Recital 33, Recital 42, Article 4(11), Article 5(1)(b), Article 6(1)(a), Article 9(2)(a).

Further reading cited in original brief: WP29 Guidelines on consent (WP259); Council of Europe Recommendation CM/Rec(2019)2 on health-related data.

GA4GH GDPR Forum Policy Brief — Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) and Genomic Health Research
Source: https://www.ga4gh.org/news_item/gdpr-brief-the-data-protection-impact-assessment-and-genomic-health-research/
License: GA4GH GDPR Brief (public; not legal advice)
Tier: P1 | Jurisdiction: EU | Framework: GDPR

DISCLAIMER: Excerpted for RegBot corpus ingest. Not legal advice.

Article 35 of the GDPR requires data controllers to perform a Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) before processing personal data when processing "is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons."

Applicability to genomic research

Large-scale genomic health research involving personal data will typically require a DPIA. High-risk indicators relevant to genomics include:
- systematic and extensive evaluation of personal aspects (including genetic data);
- large-scale pro

erify that a DPIA exists or is planned before cross-border sharing of identifiable or re-identifiable genomic datasets from EU/EEA subjects.

Relevant GDPR provisions: Article 35, Article 36, Recitals 84, 90, 91.

GA4GH GDPR Forum — Primer: Ten Key GDPR Areas for International Genomic and Health-Related Data Sharing
Source: https://www.ga4gh.org/news_item/introducing-the-ga4gh-gdpr-and-international-health-data-sharing-forum/
License: GA4GH GDPR Brief (public; not legal advice — see disclaimer on source page)
Tier: P1 | Jurisdiction: EU | Framework: GDPR

DISCLAIMER: This text is excerpted for RegBot corpus ingest from GA4GH public GDPR Briefs. It does not constitute legal advice.

The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) took full legal effect across the European Union on 25 May 2018. It has significant implications for international health research involving the collection, use, and cross-border sharing of personal data, including genomics research.

The GA4GH Health Data Sharing, Privacy, and Regulatory Forum (formerly GDPR Forum) published an introductory primer highlighting ten key areas

earch.

The GA4GH Health Data Sharing, Privacy, and Regulatory Forum (formerly GDPR Forum) published an introductory primer highlighting ten key areas of the GDPR that affect international health research and genomic data sharing:

1. Territorial scope — When GDPR applies to processing activities, including research conducted outside the EU that targets or monitors individuals in the EU.

2. Transparency and accountability — Controllers must provide clear information to data subjects and demonstrate compliance with GDPR principles.

3. Data protection by design and by default — Technical and organisational measures must integrate privacy protections from the outset of processing systems.

4. Data protection impact assessments (DPIAs) — Required before processing likely to result in high risk to rights and freedoms; large-scale genomic health research typically triggers DPIA obligations.

ired before processing likely to result in high risk to rights and freedoms; large-scale genomic health research typically triggers DPIA obligations.

5. Data Protection Officers (DPOs) — Certain controllers and processors must designate a DPO with expert knowledge of data protection law and practices.

6. Consent — Must be freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous. Special rules apply when consent is the legal basis for processing genomic and health-related data (special categories under Article 9).

7. Processing special categories of personal data — Genetic data, biometric data, and data concerning health require an Article 9 lawful basis in addition to an Article 6 basis. Consent is one permitted basis; Member State law may authorise processing for scientific research subject to safeguards.

8. Data subject rights and research derogations — Rights include access, rectificatio

orise processing for scientific research subject to safeguards.

8. Data subject rights and research derogations — Rights include access, rectification, erasure, restriction, portability, and objection. Research contexts may qualify for limited derogations where rights would seriously impair research objectives, subject to conditions and safeguards.

9. Secondary use and further processing — Further processing for scientific research may be compatible with the original purpose if appropriate safeguards exist. Broad consent for research may be permissible under Recital 33 when subject to ethical standards and oversight.

10. International data transfer — Transfer of personal data outside the EU/EEA requires an adequacy decision, appropriate safeguards (e.g. Standard Contractual Clauses), or a derogation. Cross-border genomic data sharing must address transfer mechanisms explicitly.

Offic

safeguards (e.g. Standard Contractual Clauses), or a derogation. Cross-border genomic data sharing must address transfer mechanisms explicitly.

Official GDPR text: https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=CELEX:32016R0679
More GA4GH GDPR Briefs: https://www.ga4gh.org/product/gdpr-international-health-data-sharing-forum/

Mainland China — Human Genetic Resources, PIPL, and Data Security Law excerpts
Source: HGR Regulation (国务院令); PIPL; DSL — official Chinese legal databases
Tier: P2 | Jurisdiction: CN | Framework: national

DISCLAIMER: RegBot navigation excerpt for cross-border genomic sharing review. Confirm with qualified counsel and current PRC rules. Not legal advice.

Human Genetic Resources (HGR) Regulation — international cooperation and export

Human genetic resources include human genetic resource materials (organs, tissues, cells containing human genome or genes) and human genetic resource information (data from materials).

Foreign organisations or individuals, or PRC entities established or controlled by them, generally must not collect or preserve PRC human genetic resources within China, or supply PRC HGR abroad, without approval from relevant authorities.

International scientific research

ve PRC human genetic resources within China, or supply PRC HGR abroad, without approval from relevant authorities.

International scientific research cooperation involving HGR typically requires: (1) approval/filing with science and health authorities; (2) cooperation agreement with a PRC partner institution; (3) ethics review; (4) informed consent from donors; (5) plans for benefit sharing and data security.

Export or open provision of HGR information to foreign parties may require security review and compliance with cross-border data transfer rules. Clinical studies, biobank sharing, and cloud analysis on overseas servers trigger HGR and cybersecurity assessments.

Personal Information Protection Law (PIPL) — sensitive personal information

Genomic data, health data, and biometric data are often classified as sensitive personal information. Processing requires specific purpose, necess

on

Genomic data, health data, and biometric data are often classified as sensitive personal information. Processing requires specific purpose, necessity, and strict protection measures; separate consent may be required unless another lawful basis applies.

Cross-border transfer of personal information: requires one of — (a) security assessment organised by CAC; (b) personal information protection certification; (c) standard contract with overseas recipient; (d) other mechanisms prescribed by law. Critical information infrastructure operators and large-scale processors face heightened obligations.

Data Security Law (DSL)

Establishes classified protection for data by importance and national security impact. Important data and core data in life sciences sectors may face localisation, security assessment, and export controls. Research platforms combining genomic and clinical datasets shou

life sciences sectors may face localisation, security assessment, and export controls. Research platforms combining genomic and clinical datasets should document data classification and governance.

RegBot review prompts for China-origin or China-accessible genomic data

- Is HGR administrative approval/filing in place for collection, cooperation, or outbound sharing?
- Does consent cover international partners and specified uses?
- Which PIPL cross-border transfer mechanism applies?
- Are DSL/Cybersecurity Law security assessments required before cloud or foreign analysis?

Hong Kong — PDPO and cross-border personal data transfer excerpts
Source: Personal Data (Privacy) Ordinance (Cap. 486); PCPD guidance — https://www.pcpd.org.hk/
Tier: P2 | Jurisdiction: HK | Framework: national

DISCLAIMER: RegBot navigation excerpt. Section 33 not yet in force as of common PCPD guidance; verify current status. Not legal advice.

Personal Data (Privacy) Ordinance (PDPO) — data protection principles

Data Protection Principle 1 (Purpose and manner of collection): personal data must be collected for lawful purpose directly related to a function or activity, and data subjects informed of purpose and classes of transferees.

Data Protection Principle 3 (Use): personal data must not be used for new purpose without prescribed consent unless permitted exception applies.

Data Protection Principle 4 (Security): practicable steps to protect personal data against unauthorised or a

sent unless permitted exception applies.

Data Protection Principle 4 (Security): practicable steps to protect personal data against unauthorised or accidental access, processing, erasure, or other use.

Sensitive data in genomic research: health and genetic information require clear consent and purpose limitation. Hong Kong Genome Project and hospital research programmes typically use ethics committee review and participant information sheets specifying sharing scope.

Section 33 — cross-border transfer (transfer limitation)

PDPO Section 33 would prohibit transfer of personal data outside Hong Kong unless the destination jurisdiction is specified by the Commissioner as having adequate protection, or specified safeguards exist, or the data subject has consented in writing to the transfer.

Section 33 has not been brought into force; PCPD nonetheless recommends voluntary adoption of tran

subject has consented in writing to the transfer.

Section 33 has not been brought into force; PCPD nonetheless recommends voluntary adoption of transfer safeguards via model contractual clauses and due diligence for overseas processors (including cloud genomic analysis platforms).

PCPD Practical Guidance — model clauses and due diligence

Recommended approach before offshore transfer: assess recipient jurisdiction; execute PCPD-recommended model clauses; conduct due diligence on recipient security practices; document purpose and retention; notify data subjects where appropriate.

Mainland China transfers may involve both PDPO (HK) and PIPL (PRC) requirements when data flows between HK and mainland entities.

RegBot review prompts for Hong Kong-origin genomic data

- Consent and PICS (personal information collection statement) cover overseas research partners and cloud regions?
- If re

g Kong-origin genomic data

- Consent and PICS (personal information collection statement) cover overseas research partners and cloud regions?
- If relying on voluntary s.33 safeguards, are model clauses and due diligence files complete?
- Dual compliance if data flows HK ↔ mainland or HK ↔ EU/US?
- Ethics committee approval for secondary use and linkage with EMR/genomic records?

Japan — research ethics guidelines, APPI, and Genomic Medicine Act excerpts
Source: MHLW ethical guidelines; APPI; Act on Promotion of Genomic Medicine (2023)
Tier: P2 | Jurisdiction: JP | Framework: national

DISCLAIMER: RegBot navigation excerpt. Not legal advice.

Ethical Guidelines for Medical and Health Research Involving Human Subjects

Joint MEXT/MHLW/METI guidelines govern ethical conduct of medical and health research. Genomic research involving human samples or identifiable data requires review by an certified research ethics committee (REC/IRB).

Informed consent must explain purpose, methods, risks, and whether samples/data may be stored for future research or shared with third parties domestically or abroad. Broad consent for unspecified future research is permitted under conditions including ethics oversight and public benefit.

Opt-out and information provision models appl

nspecified future research is permitted under conditions including ethics oversight and public benefit.

Opt-out and information provision models apply in limited circumstances for archival data; cross-border sharing generally expects explicit consent or legal authorisation documented in ethics review.

Act on the Protection of Personal Information (APPI) — cross-border transfer

Personal information handlers must specify purpose of use and obtain consent where required. Personal data requiring consideration (including health information) needs heightened care.

Transfer of personal data to third parties in foreign countries requires: (1) consent after informing data subject of foreign privacy system and safeguards; or (2) recipient in country with equivalent protection; or (3) standard contractual clauses approved by PPC; or (4) binding corporate rules, among other mechanisms.

Anonymis

try with equivalent protection; or (3) standard contractual clauses approved by PPC; or (4) binding corporate rules, among other mechanisms.

Anonymised or pseudonymised genomic datasets may still be personal information if re-identification is possible; cloud analysis outside Japan triggers transfer rules.

Act on Promotion of Genomic Medicine (2023)

Establishes national framework for safe genomic medicine and research infrastructure (including GeMJ platform). Emphasises secure data linkage, quality-controlled sharing for research and clinical application, and coordination across designated medical institutions.

Major biobanks: Biobank Japan, Tohoku Megabank, NCBN — access via material/data transfer agreements and ethics approval.

RegBot review prompts for Japan-origin genomic data

- REC approval covers international collaboration and cloud jurisdiction?
- APPI transfer mechanism do

t review prompts for Japan-origin genomic data

- REC approval covers international collaboration and cloud jurisdiction?
- APPI transfer mechanism documented (consent disclosure, SCC, adequacy)?
- If using broad/future-use consent, are ethics conditions and public information duties met?
- GeMJ or hospital MTA/DUA limits compatible with researcher protocol?

South Korea — Bioethics and Safety Act and PIPA excerpts
Source: Bioethics and Safety Act; Personal Information Protection Act — https://elaw.klri.re.kr/
Tier: P2 | Jurisdiction: KR | Framework: national

DISCLAIMER: RegBot navigation excerpt. Not legal advice.

Bioethics and Safety Act — human subject research and genetic data

Regulates human subject research, embryo/fetal research limits, and handling of human-derived materials including genetic information. Research protocols typically require institutional review board (IRB) approval.

Collection and use of human tissues, genetic information, and personal data for research must align with informed consent and IRB conditions. Broad or future-use consent is permitted subject to IRB oversight and statutory requirements.

Provision of human-derived materials or genetic information to third parties, including foreign institutions, genera

ight and statutory requirements.

Provision of human-derived materials or genetic information to third parties, including foreign institutions, generally requires consent scope covering such transfer, IRB approval, and compliance with export/security rules where applicable.

National programmes (NBK, CODA, K-BDS, BIKO) operate under ministry oversight with standardised access agreements for domestic and international researchers.

Personal Information Protection Act (PIPA) — cross-border transfer

Personal information controllers must obtain data subject consent for cross-border transfer unless an exception applies, and inform subjects of recipient, purpose, items transferred, and retention period.

Sensitive information (including health and potentially genetic data) requires separate consent for collection and use unless another basis exists. Pseudonymisation and technical safeguards a

nd potentially genetic data) requires separate consent for collection and use unless another basis exists. Pseudonymisation and technical safeguards are expected for large-scale genomic databases.

Data Protection Commission (PIPC) guidance emphasises accountability, impact assessment for high-risk processing, and documented contracts with overseas processors.

Framework Act on AI (2025/2026) — emerging relevance

High-risk AI using health or biometric data may face additional transparency and risk-management duties; genomic analysis pipelines should be reviewed for alignment with AI governance rules.

RegBot review prompts for Korea-origin genomic data

- Valid IRB approval and consent for proposed sharing or foreign access?
- PIPA cross-border consent and disclosure to participants completed?
- For NBK/CODA access, do DAC conditions match researcher DUL and consent scope?
- Any biosafe

order consent and disclosure to participants completed?
- For NBK/CODA access, do DAC conditions match researcher DUL and consent scope?
- Any biosafety or export control issues for physical samples vs data-only transfer?

Singapore — PDPA and HBRA excerpts for genomic and health research
Source: PDPA 2012 (https://sso.agc.gov.sg/Act/PDPA2012); HBRA 2015 (https://sso.agc.gov.sg/Act/HBRA2015)
Tier: P2 | Jurisdiction: SG | Framework: national

DISCLAIMER: RegBot navigation excerpt; verify against current statutes and PDPC/HBRA guidance. Not legal advice.

Personal Data Protection Act 2012 (PDPA) — key themes for research data

Organisations must collect, use, and disclose personal data only for purposes a reasonable person would consider appropriate, with consent or another permitted basis. Personal data includes data that can identify an individual, alone or with other information.

Consent: must be informed and voluntary. Organisations should tell individuals the purposes of collection, use, and disclosure, including any likely cross-border transfer. Withdrawal of consent should be facilitated where applic

the purposes of collection, use, and disclosure, including any likely cross-border transfer. Withdrawal of consent should be facilitated where applicable.

Transfer limitation obligation: organisations must not transfer personal data outside Singapore unless the receiving country or territory ensures a comparable standard of protection, or the individual has given consent after being informed of risks, or other PDPA exceptions apply. Contractual clauses and binding corporate rules are common safeguards for international research collaborations.

Protection obligation and retention: reasonable security arrangements; retain personal data only as long as necessary for legal or business purposes.

Human Biomedical Research Act (HBRA) — human biomedical research oversight

Regulates conduct of human biomedical research in Singapore. Research involving human subjects, biological material, or

iomedical research oversight

Regulates conduct of human biomedical research in Singapore. Research involving human subjects, biological material, or personal information derived from such research may require ethics committee review and compliance with HBRA regulations.

Research institutions and investigators must obtain appropriate consent for collection and use of human biological material and associated data. Secondary use and sharing for research may require ethics approval and alignment with original consent scope.

Health Information Bill (HIB) — emerging framework

Singapore is developing unified health information governance. Researchers sharing genomic or clinical data across healthcare and research settings should monitor HIB requirements for data sharing, custodianship, and national health information exchange.

RegBot review prompts for Singapore-origin genomic data

- Was

quirements for data sharing, custodianship, and national health information exchange.

RegBot review prompts for Singapore-origin genomic data

- Was PDPA consent (or deemed/other basis) obtained for collection, use, disclosure, and overseas transfer?
- Does HBRA ethics approval cover proposed secondary use or international sharing?
- Are transfer safeguards documented (DPA, SCC-equivalent, participant notification)?
- Is re-identification risk from genomic data addressed in security and access controls?

Taiwan — Human Biobank Management Act and PDPA excerpts
Source: Human Biobank Management Act; Personal Data Protection Act — https://law.moj.gov.tw/
Tier: P2 | Jurisdiction: TW | Framework: national

DISCLAIMER: RegBot navigation excerpt. Not legal advice.

Human Biobank Management Act — biobank establishment and use

Governs establishment, management, and use of human biobanks and protection of participants. A biobank must obtain approval and register with competent authority before operation.

Informed consent is required for collection of biological specimens and related personal data. Consent should specify purposes, types of use, duration, and whether data may be used for other research or provided to third parties.

Participants may withdraw consent; biobanks must establish procedures for withdrawal and handling of retained materials and data subject to legal retention limits.

Use

ithdraw consent; biobanks must establish procedures for withdrawal and handling of retained materials and data subject to legal retention limits.

Use of biobank data and specimens for research requires review by an ethics committee. Transfer to domestic or foreign researchers may require additional consent, ethics approval, and contractual safeguards depending on scope.

Personal Data Protection Act (PDPA) — cross-border transfer

Personal data processors must implement appropriate security measures. When a non-government agency transfers personal data internationally, it must ensure the recipient country has adequate protection, or obtain consent from the data subject after providing necessary information, unless an exception applies.

Sensitive personal information (including medical and genetic data in practice) warrants heightened notice and security. Secondary use beyond original c

nsitive personal information (including medical and genetic data in practice) warrants heightened notice and security. Secondary use beyond original collection purpose may require new consent or legal basis.

Major programmes: Taiwan Biobank, TPMI, NBCT — harmonisation through shared standards for cross-biobank linkage.

RegBot review prompts for Taiwan-origin genomic data

- Is the providing entity a registered biobank or study with valid ethics approval?
- Does consent cover proposed secondary use, linkage with EMR, or foreign collaborator access?
- For cross-border transfer, which PDPA Article 21 mechanism applies (adequacy, consent, contract)?
- Are participant withdrawal and return-of-results policies documented?
